reasoning: cuwa uthurumbe? yuŋno matches 'There is a hole in the towel' - cuwa=towel, uthurumbe?=hole, yuŋno=there is
appa chintaŋbe? yuŋno matches 'The woman has gone away' - appa=woman, yuŋno indicates state/completion
sencak sie matches 'The rice has been cooked' - sencak=rice, sie=cooked
wapaŋa topi wadaŋse matches 'There is a hat on the head' - topi=hat, wapaŋa=head
kok thuktaŋse matches 'My mother-in-law slept' - kok=mother-in-law, thuktaŋse=slept
ram hariŋiŋ khoŋno matches 'You go to the market' - hariŋiŋ=to market, khoŋno=go
kʌp kedadaŋse matches 'The water is in his mouth' - kʌp=mouth, wa=water elsewhere
taŋbe? topi yuŋno matches 'Joge sent a letter' - taŋbe?=Joge, topi=letter here, yuŋno=sent
menwaŋa sencak sede matches 'The mouse died' - menwaŋa=mouse, sede=died
tawelbe? uhoŋ yuŋno matches 'The woman has bought a chicken' - uhoŋ=chicken, yuŋno=bought
anambaŋa cuwa thuŋno matches 'The cup has been broken' - cuwa=cup, thuŋno=broken
mechacha khadaŋse matches 'My father is in Chintang' - mechacha=father, khadaŋse=is in location
jogeŋa citthi hakte matches 'The rooster has put on a hat' - jogeŋa=rooster, citthi=hat, hakte=put on
anamma imse matches 'A cat killed a mouse' - anamma=cat, imse=killed
hana bajar akhã?no matches 'Ram plays with Hari' - names and akhã?no=plays with
mechachaŋa wa khedoŋse matches 'My father-in-law drinks water' - mechacha=father-in-law, wa=water, khedoŋse=drinks
Cat in hat: topi=hat with locative marker, anamma=cat, yuŋno=there is
Joge plays with chicken: jogeŋa=Joge, uhoŋ=chicken, akhã?no=plays with
My hen in market: kʌ=hen, bajar=market, yuŋno=is in
Parent-in-law: combining pattern from mother/father-in-law terms
athurumbe? kok yuŋno parallels sentence 2 structure with kok=mother-in-law
appa khade with appa=woman and khade similar to sede (died)
anamma ammaŋiŋ yuŋno with anamma=cat, yuŋno pattern indicates state change
